articles.title: On the Radar: Brazil’s ‘Escobar,’ Haiti’s Latest Massacre, and Venezuela’s Former First Lady Under Scrutiny
articles.description: This week’s On the Radar highlights Haiti’s latest massacre, Brazil’s “Pablo Escobar,” and the legal troubles of Venezuela’s former first lady.

articles.title: The Many Lives of ‘Iván Mordisco,’ Colombia’s Most Wanted
articles.description: President Gustavo Petro has intensified operations against Iván Mordisco, Colombia’s most wanted criminal. But, after years of military offensives and false reports of his death, the guerrilla leader continues to elude authorities.

articles.title: From Airstrikes to Cooperation: Will the ‘New Phase’ of Ecuador’s Drug War Deliver?
articles.description: Ecuador’s revamping of old security measures like curfews and mass arrests of suspected gang collaborators are now being complemented with airstrikes backed by the United States and operations against transatlantic drug trafficking organizations in tandem with European agencies. But will this

articles.title: The Corruption Engine Behind Sebastián Marset’s Empire
articles.description: A Paraguayan senator was convicted for laundering cocaine profits through real estate and a soccer club, and supporting trafficking via his private plane — exposing how trafficker Sebastián Marset’s alleged network infiltrated politics and business across the region.

articles.title: On the Radar: Cocaine Power Shifts and Seizures
articles.description: Allegations link Colombia’s President Gustavo Petro to drug trafficking, while Venezuela’s regime, and its Cartel of the Suns, process a major personnel shift. In Chile, a major cocaine and ketamine seizure raises eyebrows.

articles.title: Map: Military Strikes Target Complex Criminal Landscape on Colombia-Ecuador Border
articles.description: InSight Crime maps the complex criminal dynamics at play in the Colombia-Ecuador border region, one of the world’s most important cocaine transit corridors.

articles.title: On the Radar: Latin America Keeps Attacking Crime — But Is It Working?
articles.description: Bombings on Ecuador’s border with Colombia, the arrest of one of the region’s most wanted traffickers, and more hardline anti-crime measures from Chile are the focus this week. But is any of it working?

articles.title: What Does the Exit of Vladimir Padrino López Mean for Organized Crime in Venezuela?
articles.description: What does the dismissal of Vladimir Padrino López mean for criminal dynamics in Venezuela and the future of the Cartel of the Suns?

articles.title: Dollarino and the Rise of Brokers in the Cocaine Pipeline to Europe
articles.description: The upcoming trial of Emanuele Gregorini in Italy offers an inside look at the growing role that brokers play in connecting drug trafficking networks in Europe and Latin America.

articles.title: How Fentanyl Was Introduced in Mexico’s Local Drug Markets
articles.description: As Mexican criminal organizations became the main suppliers of illicit fentanyl to the United States, small pockets of domestic consumption began to emerge along key trafficking corridors. Since at least […]

articles.title: The Rise and Fall of Sebastián Marset
articles.description: Sebastián Marset was one of South America’s most wanted drug traffickers prior to his arrest in Bolivia. Learn more about his criminal career.

articles.title: On the Radar: What’s New in Trump’s Push for More Military Might to Fight Organized Crime?
articles.description: The founder of one of Mexico’s most powerful criminal groups pleads guilty, US President Trump’s new regional anti-drug push aims to up military cooperation against organized crime in the region, and nearly 50 people are injured in a nightclub grenade attack in Peru.

articles.title: 5 Things to Know About the Capture of Sebastián Marset
articles.description: Sebastián Marset was one of South America’s most wanted drug traffickers before his recent arrest in Bolivia, but how did the Uruguayan criminal leader evade capture for so long?

articles.title: Sebastián Marset
articles.description: Sebastián Marset is Uruguay’s most famous drug trafficker and the leader of an international network known as the First Uruguayan Cartel (Primer Cartel Uruguayo – PCU).

articles.title: Police Crisis Tests Security Strategy in Rosario, Argentina’s Organized Crime Hub
articles.description: The government’s security model for Rosario, Argentina’s most violent city, was seemingly showing results and bringing down homicide rates, but local police started to burn out.

articles.title: Amid US Strikes, Booming Guyana Could Become Drug Trafficking Hub
articles.description: US military intervention in and near Venezuela could be displacing cocaine trafficking to other parts of the region, notably Guyana, whose new seaports and rapid economic growth increase its attractiveness for drug traffickers and organized crime.

articles.title: Breaking Analysis: LatAm Militaries Are Already Fighting ‘Cartels,’ But Trump Wants More
articles.description: US President Donald Trump says the only way to defeat organized crime, drug trafficking, and transnational gangs is by using military force.

articles.title: On the Radar: Ecuador’s Escalating Crime Crisis
articles.description: El Mencho’s lavish funeral in Mexico, why violence is rising in Ecuador despite hard-line security measures, and the future of the South American country’s fight against organized crime. are the focus of this week’s roundup of the main organized crime stories in Latin America.

articles.title: More Than Narco Wives: Women’s Hidden Power in Latin American Organized Crime
articles.description: Women continue to be a fundamental part of Latin America’s organized crime groups and have held high-ranking positions in the drug business.

articles.title: Is Brazil’s Biggest Port Winning the Fight Against Drug Traffickers?
articles.description: Authorities at Brazil’s Port of Santos are analyzing new intelligence and data to identify how drug traffickers operate and intercept them in the act.

articles.title: On the Radar: El Mencho’s Capture – Gamechanger or Business as Usual?
articles.description: The death of Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, alias “El Mencho,” leader of the Jalisco Cartel New Generation, shook Mexico’s criminal landscape. In On the Radar, we analyze the impact of his fall and the different scenarios facing the Jalisco Cartel New Generation.

articles.title: The Future of Drug Trafficking Out of Venezuela
articles.description: Drug trafficking out of Venezuela predates Nicolás Maduro and will long outlive him. The question is whether the cocaine trade will continue to be managed and regulated by the Chavista regime, or whether Maduro’s removal will weaken state-embedded drug trafficking.

articles.title: Camaraderie and Drug Trafficking: Colombian Guerrillas in Venezuela
articles.description: The ELN, in cooperation with Venezuelan authorities, has used several tactics to consolidate its control along the Colombia-Venezuela border. This binational boundary is crossed by countless drug trafficking corridors, an illegal economy that has financed both high-ranking regime officials and Colom

articles.title: The Evolution of the Cartel of the Suns
articles.description: The  Cartel of the Suns, while perhaps debilitated after Maduro’s capture, is still very much alive. What’s more, after numerous transformations over many decades, it looks set to undergo another transformation.

articles.title: The Criminal Portfolio Sustaining Military Loyalty in Venezuela
articles.description: For many members of Venezuela’s armed forces, drug trafficking, illegal mining and other criminal rents have become a parallel source of income that offset meager wages.

articles.title: The Shifting US Portrait of the Cartel of the Suns
articles.description: The United States justified the Maduro arrest as a law enforcement action intended to take down the leader of a powerful criminal organization. But US officials have offered evolving and sometimes contradictory descriptions of the so-called cartel that Maduro allegedly headed.

articles.title: The King Is Dead, Long Live the Queen
articles.description: The state-embedded drug trafficking structure in Venezuela is largely intact, although the conditions under which it operates have shifted dramatically.

articles.title: CJNG Violence After the Killing of ‘El Mencho’: Power Play or Last Gasp?
articles.description: The government’s killing of “El Mencho” on February 22 provoked a spasm of violence by his cartel that has been, in some ways, unprecedented. But previous displays of force by aggrieved criminal groups have proven to be largely temporary and, in most instances, a last gasp before sputtering and atom

articles.title: What’s Next for Mexico’s CJNG After the Killing of ‘El Mencho’?
articles.description: Following El Mencho’s death, the CJNG faces a new era of succession and potential fragmentation. Who will lead the cartel and its criminal operations in Mexico now?

articles.title: Jalisco Cartel New Generation (CJNG)
articles.description: The Jalisco Cartel New Generation is often considered Mexico’s most feared cartel, whose operations stretch across the country.

articles.title: Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, alias ‘El Mencho’
articles.description: Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, alias “El Mencho,” has propelled the Jalisco Cartel’s growth to become a fierce and powerful criminal network.

articles.title: US Sanctions in Latin America Have Increased. But Can They Be Enforced?
articles.description: A wave of sanctions on drug traffickers and their associates has been compromised by a lag in enforcement, limiting the impact on transnational criminal networks.

articles.title: On the Radar: Football, Cocaine, and Corruption
articles.description: How football fuels Paraguay’s drug trade, the latest extradition request from Mexico to the US, and what the latest cocaine seizure in El Salvador reveals are the focus of this week’s roundup of the main organized crime stories in Latin America.

articles.title: How Guatemala’s Gang Crisis Reached a Boiling Point
articles.description:
Guatemala is returning to hardline security policies amid a resurgence of gang activity, two years after its president pledged a renewed approach to the problem. 
 But in attempting to target gang leadership, the government has exposed its limited capacity to do so.

articles.title: The State of Play: US Anti-Drug Cooperation in Latin America
articles.description: Regional cooperation with the United States on anti-narcotics is a central focus of US policy in Latin America. This interactive map shows the current role of each country in drug trafficking and the status of their anti-narcotics cooperation with the United States.

articles.title: On the Radar: Power, Corruption, and Criminality
articles.description: This week, we’re asking what the future might hold for the United States’ relationship with Honduras, what a mass kidnapping in Mexico tells us about the country’s crime wars, and whether another high-level political arrest in Ecuador signals progress in the fight against corruptio

articles.title: Colombia’s EMC Holds ‘Total Peace’ Hostage in Cauca
articles.description: At the heart of the conflict in Cauca is control of territory peppered with illegal gold mines, coca and marijuana crops, and a bridge connecting Colombia to the Pacific – a key exit point for drugs heading to international markets.

articles.title: Video: Are ICE’s Roundups in the United States an Effective Anti-Crime Strategy?
articles.description: The Trump administration has used the threat of organized crime to justify roundups of suspected undocumented migrants. InSight Crime’s Managing Editor Deborah Bonello and Senior Investigator James Bargent discuss the findings of our recent investigation into immigration enforcement operations in Na

articles.title: Nicolás Maduro
articles.description: The former president of Venezuela, Nicolás Maduro, oversaw the consolidation of a hybrid state in which governance and criminality became deeply entwined, transforming the country into a regional hub for organized crime.

articles.title: On the Radar: Presidents Talk Organized Crime
articles.description: Presidents Trump and Petro meet at the White House, Brazil’s Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva warns of AI’s potential to be weaponized by organized crime for elections, and Costa Rica’s president-elect Laura Fernández promises to be tough on crime.

articles.title: Will the US Send Alleged Fuel Smugglers to Mexico?
articles.description: Mexico’s extradition request for several American citizens accused of smuggling stolen oil exposes an imbalance in the US-Mexico bilateral security relationship.

articles.title: On the Radar: Violence from Gangs and Governments Rules the Region
articles.description: Gang massacres in Mexico, more hardline measures against drug-related violence in Ecuador, and legal action from the victims of Trump’s missile strikes in the Caribbean dominated the conversations around organized crime in the Americas this week. Here’s our take.

articles.title: Costa Rica’s Leading Presidential Candidates Embrace Hardline Security
articles.description: There are 19 candidates in the running, and polls show one, Laura Fernández, as the clear frontrunner. All the leading contenders’ security proposals largely point toward hardline measures, including expanding the police force, strengthening port security, and imposing harsher penalties, underscorin

articles.title: FARC Dissidents in Venezuela
articles.description: Venezuela is a vital base of operations for the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC), and three of its seven blocs (fighting divisions) have a presence there. The country provides the guerrillas with key drug trafficking corridors, a place to escape pressure from Colombian security for

articles.title: Organized Crime and AI: 5 Topics We Are Monitoring in 2026
articles.description: As advances in artificial intelligence bring it to nearly all corners of life, both criminal organizations and law enforcement are adopting these new technologies to try and gain an edge. These are the five areas we are watching most closely in 2026.

articles.title: Will Colombian Guerrillas Lose Their Safe Haven in Post‑Maduro Venezuela?
articles.description: The combination of former Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro’s exit from power and increasing US military pressure has disrupted the criminal ecosystem in Venezuela, putting strain on Colombian guerrilla groups that have long benefitted from their ties to Chavismo and which now may retreat in

articles.title: On the Radar: Mexico Responds to US Pressure
articles.description: Violent gang reprisals in Guatemala, a symbolic push for justice in Colombia, and mounting pressure on Mexico from the United States are in the spotlight this week. Dive into the latest developments shaping the region’s security landscape.

articles.title: Álex Saab
articles.description: Álex Nain Saab Moran is a Colombian businessman accused of being one of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro’s main frontmen. International authorities accuse Saab of taking part in a multimillion-dollar corruption […]

articles.title: How InSight Crime Tracked Down an Explosive Narco Video in Honduras
articles.description: On September 3, 2024, InSight Crime published a video showing Carlos Zelaya, the brother-in-law of Honduran President Xiomara Castro, in a room full of the country’s most powerful drug traffickers. This is the story of how we got that video.

articles.title: Cartel of the Suns
articles.description: The “Cartel of the Suns” (Cartel de los Soles) is the term used to describe the shadowy groups inside Venezuela’s military that are involved in a wide range of criminal activities, including gasoline smuggling, illegal mining and other corruption schemes, but primarily drug trafficking.

articles.title: On the Radar: The Ripples of Maduro’s Removal Continue
articles.description: This week, we analyze the ripples of recent US operations in the Caribbean and Venezuela across the region. That includes tensions between the US and Mexico, violence along the Colombia–Venezuela border, and the continuation of record cocaine seizures between Latin America and Europe.

articles.title: ELN in Venezuela
articles.description: The National Liberation Army (Ejército de Liberación Nacional – ELN) is the last active insurgency in Colombia, and the main criminal actor in the Colombia-Venezuela border. From there, the group has expanded into Venezuelan territory.

articles.title: 5 Criminal Figures Who Helped Develop Venezuela’s System of Corruption
articles.description:
The arrest of former Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro and his wife on drug trafficking charges on January 3 brought into sharp focus the involvement of corrupt Venezuelan state elements in drug trafficking. 
Here are five figures who played a prominent role in the development of Venezuelan state

articles.title: Tareck El Aissami
articles.description: Tareck El Aissami was Venezuela’s Vice President of the Economy and the country’s Minister of Industries and National Production until March 20, 2023, when he resigned after an anti-corruption operation arrested multiple high-ranking officials in his circle.

articles.title: National Liberation Army (ELN)
articles.description: The National Liberation Army (Ejército de Liberación Nacional – ELN) is Colombia’s last true insurgency and one of Latin America’s most powerful criminal organizations.

articles.title: Report Puts International Banks, Honduras Elites at Center of Berta Cáceres Murder
articles.description: The murder of renowned Indigenous activist and environmental defender Berta Cáceres in Honduras in 2016 was an organized criminal operation funded by money from two international development banks that was diverted away from a controversial extractive project to pay for illegal activities and murder

articles.title: The Colectivos’ Role in the Struggle for Power in Post-Maduro Venezuela
articles.description:
Tension has flooded Caracas since the early hours of January 3, when then-President Nicolás Maduro and his wife were arrested and taken to prison in the United States. In response, armed men with their faces covered by balaclavas poured into the capital’s streets. 
 These men are members of colectiv

articles.title: On the Radar: Venezuela’s New Criminal Chapter
articles.description: Who is going to control the country’s criminal economies following the exit of Nicolás Maduro? You can find profiles of groups, including the ELN and colectivos, as well as players such as Diosdado Cabello, in our archive.

articles.title: 5 Crime Groups You Need to Know to Understand Post-Maduro Venezuela
articles.description:
The arrest of Nicolás Maduro marks a new chapter for organized crime in Venezuela. The country’s slew of criminal groups will have to adjust their operations and alliances amid a political shakeup that is certain to have impacts across the underworld.  
 These are five of the country’s most pr

articles.title: GameChangers 2025: US Reignites the War on Drugs and Weakens It for 2026
articles.description: The quantum shift in US security policy in Latin America and the Caribbean under President Donald Trump in 2025 has moved towards a militarized and repressive model and is forsaking the most successful elements of the fight against transnational organized crime: cooperation and intelligence sharing.

articles.title: How Maduro’s Ouster Will Shift Criminal Dynamics in Venezuela
articles.description: US President Donald Trump insisted that the capture of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro has removed the “kingpin of a vast criminal network,” but it will not upend the Venezuelan government’s permissive stance toward organized crime.

articles.title: 3 Criminal Consequences of Maduro’s Exit From Venezuela
articles.description: The dramatic arrest of Venezuela’s President Nicolás Maduro this weekend poses three key questions about the future of the criminal groups and dynamics in the South American country and Latin America.

articles.title: GameChangers 2025: Colombia’s Total Peace Remains in Pieces
articles.description: In 2025, Colombia faced levels of violence not seen in years, driven by clashes among criminal groups that have strengthened their military power and territorial control in areas critical to illegal economies.This historical backdrop is essential to understanding how Colombia’s security dynamics may

articles.title: GameChangers 2025: Migration Smuggling Market Goes From Boom to Bust
articles.description: The number of migrants traveling through Latin America to the United States plummeted in 2025 and sent organized crime groups scrambling to fill a huge hole in their profit. The abrupt turnaround is the result of major policy changes in the United States, which have  have brought a crashing halt to

articles.title: GameChangers 2025: How the Trump Administration Reset the Fight Against Organized Crime
articles.description: From drug boat bombings to terrorist designations to WMDs, in 2025, the Trump administration upended the way the United States confronts criminals, but its impact on organized crime going forward is far from clear.

articles.title: What Happens Now That the US Has Labeled Fentanyl a WMD
articles.description: US President Donald Trump has designated the synthetic drug fentanyl as a weapon of mass destruction, putting the drug on par with nuclear bombs and weaponized biological agents, and further propelling the war on drugs to the forefront of his national security agenda.

articles.title: How Mexico’s Fuel Theft Market Went Transnational
articles.description: Mexico’s largest criminal groups are expanding the sophistication and profitability of their illicit fuel markets beyond local theft into cross-border smuggling networks that affect the US energy sector.

articles.title: Corruption Looms Over Record Marijuana Seizure in Paraguay
articles.description: Forty police officers are under investigation after Paraguay’s anti-drug unit made a record-breaking marijuana seizure, in a case that underscores how endemic corruption has historically frustrated anti-trafficking operations and sowed distrust among authorities.

articles.title: What a ‘Historic’ Marijuana Seizure Says About Smuggling Dynamics in Argentina
articles.description: A recent seizure of nearly 10 tons of marijuana in Argentina was portrayed by the government as an historic blow to organized crime, but it actually highlighted a recurring smuggling dynamic on the South American country’s northern border.

articles.title: Border Wars: The ELN and the Takeover of Catatumbo
articles.description: The ELN offensive was also the last straw for the peace process with the government of President Gustavo Petro. Following the Catatumbo incident, the Colombian government suspended dialogue with the ELN, leaving a rift that is still unmended. And with presidential elections next year, it likely deal

articles.title: The Future of the ELN
articles.description: The future of the National Liberation Army (Ejército de Liberación Nacional – ELN) is now intimately connected to Venezuela. As long as the Venezuelan government supports the Colombian rebel group, it cannot be fully defeated and is unlikely to sign any peace agreement.

articles.title: Guerrilla Governance: The ELN’s Social Control in Táchira
articles.description: The ELN’s impunity in Táchira is the result of years of complicity and cooperation with local security forces and pro-government leaders. In many areas, the ELN has gained such profound social control that citizens view the group as an extension of the state – perhaps even more reliable than Preside

articles.title: Cocaine Corridor: Drugs Drive ELN’s Venezuela Expansion
articles.description: The ELN has now reportedly expanded to operating its own cocaine laboratories and overseeing transport of the drug across borders. The group’s seizure of Colombia’s coca-rich Catatumbo border region in early 2025 was a big step in a wider, planned expansion into the drug trade, likely in tandem with

articles.title: Could Armed Groups Backed by Maduro Resist a US Invasion of Venezuela?
articles.description: With the United States weighing up a military intervention to remove Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro from power, the criminal organizations aligned with the regime could soon face a critical choice: protect their economic interests or stand by their political patrons.

articles.title: InSight Crime Managing Editor Highlights Role of Women in Mexico’s Underworld
articles.description: InSight Crime Managing Editor Deborah Bonello was recently interviewed by the major Mexican newspaper Milenio to discuss her book, “Narcas: The Secret Rise of Women in Latin America’s Cartels,” which was just published in Spanish.

articles.title: Chapitos Plea Deal Raises More Questions on US Role in El Mayo’s Kidnapping in Mexico
articles.description: A top leader of the Chapitos faction of the Sinaloa Cartel has agreed to cooperate with US prosecutors, marking yet another judicial case that will not go to trial or expose the inner workings of organized crime in Mexico.

articles.title: The Drug Case Against the Former Honduras President Trump Pardoned
articles.description: US President Donald Trump’s pardon of former Honduran President Juan Orlando Hernández belies the evidence US prosecutors used to convict the former head of state on cocaine trafficking charges.

articles.title: Ecuador’s Official Security Claims Belie Reality
articles.description: Ecuador’s government marked two years in office by trumpeting the results of its hardline anti-crime policies. But its claims of progress don’t present a clear picture of the country’s security realities.

articles.title: The Pros and Cons of a US Intervention in Venezuela
articles.description: The world has been watching to see if the United States will escalate its militarized anti-drug mission in the Caribbean into a direct intervention aimed at toppling the regime of  Venezuela’s President Nicolás Maduro, prompting intense debate about the wisdom and potential impacts of such an action

articles.title: What’s Behind Costa Rica’s ‘First’ Transnational Criminal Organization?
articles.description: Authorities in Costa Rica are on high alert after uncovering the so-called South Caribbean Cartel, reportedly the country’s first transnational criminal organization, which may have severe implications for security policy in the Central American nation amid escalating violence and corruption s

articles.title: InSight Crime Co-director Adds Nuance to ‘Cartel of the Suns’ Debate
articles.description: After the US State Department announced it would designate Venezuela’s Cartel of the Suns as a “foreign terrorist organization,” InSight Crime Co-director Jeremy McDermott was interviewed by major US news organizations like the New York Times and CNN to clarify what this network wi

articles.title: On the Radar: Rebel Leader Iván Mordisco Threatens Violence After Colombia Military Strikes
articles.description: This week in On the Radar, we analyze the arrest in Spain of one of Ecuador’s most powerful criminal leaders, a major seizure of illegal gold in Paraguay, and the influence of Iván Mordisco, the leader of the Central General Staff, one of the main FARC dissident factions in Colombia.

articles.title: Top Leader of Ecuador’s Lobos Gang Arrested in Spain
articles.description: In a joint operation, authorities arrested the top leader of the Lobos, Wilmer Chavarría, alias “Pipo,” in the Spanish city of Málaga.

articles.title: InSight Crime Co-director Analyzes ELN Role in Colombia’s Cocaine Trade
articles.description: InSight Crime Co-director Jeremy McDermott was interviewed by Germany’s DW News to expand on the role of the ELN guerrillas in Colombia’s drug trade.

articles.title: Chile Election Highlights Voters’ Split on Anti-Crime Policies
articles.description: The first round of Chile’s presidential election was marked by divided opinions on how to address insecurity and organized crime.

articles.title: On the Radar: US Strikes Trigger International Rebuke
articles.description: This week in On the Radar, Ecuador inaugurates a new maximum-security mega-prison, while Colombia and the United Kingdom push back against deadly US anti-drug operations.

articles.title: The Shifting Tides of Drug Trafficking on Venezuela’s Margarita Island
articles.description: The tourist hotspot of Margarita Island, northeast of mainland Venezuela, is not just the country’s “Pearl of the Caribbean” for its idyllic beaches. It is also a strategic hub in drug trafficking routes to the Caribbean and Europe. Local fishers, criminal networks, and even security officials are i

articles.title: How Argentina’s ‘Bribe Books’ Case Could Take Down an Ex-President
articles.description: InSight Crime summarizes the key details of the so-called “Bribe Books” trial, the biggest corruption case in Argentina’s history.

articles.title: The Pulpos
articles.description: The Pulpos, also known as the Pulpos of Trujillo, are a transnational Peruvian gang dedicated to extortion, kidnapping, and murder-for-hire.

articles.title: Why Mexican Traffickers Are Leaving Zulia, Venezuela
articles.description: Major Mexican drug trafficking networks that used to have a strong presence in the state of Zulia in northwest Venezuela have left as the regime of President Nicolás Maduro has consolidated control over the country’s organized crime landscape. In an increasingly competitive criminal playing field, t

articles.title: Timeline of US Strikes Against Alleged Drug Boats
articles.description: The deadly US military strikes against alleged drug-carrying vessels in international waters mark a quantum leap and an aggressive escalation in US counternarcotics policy, in which drug trafficking is treated as a national security threat equivalent to terrorism. In order to better understand this

articles.title: Why Is the US Attacking ‘Cartels’ That Don’t Exist?
articles.description: US authorities have said recent missile strikes against alleged drug vessels in the Caribbean — and now in the Pacific — are targeting “cartels” and “narco-terrorists.” But the reality of these organizations is that they are dispersed networks that resemble most legal global supply chains, making th

articles.title: Video: How Juan Ramón Matta Ballesteros Shaped Honduras’ Drug Trade
articles.description: Honduran drug trafficker Juan Ramón Matta Ballesteros has died in a US prison at age 80, though his drug trafficking legacy lives on. Deputy director Mike LaSusa and investigator Sam Woolston discuss his career and lasting influence in the underworld.

articles.title: On the Radar: Was Brazil’s Favela Police Op a Raid or a Massacre?
articles.description: This week, we hold the line on more military strikes in the Caribbean and the Pacific, mull the conviction of a Bolivian drug czar in the US, and look at the deadliest police raid yet in a Rio favela in Brazil.

articles.title: Ghost Guns Are Increasingly Empowering Brazil’s Powerful Gangs
articles.description: A recent and extremely deadly anti-gang raid in Rio de Janeiro underscores both the escalating firepower of Brazil’s gangs and the growing challenge authorities face as criminal groups diversify their arms supplies. Trends in the use of military firearms by Brazil’s criminal organizations are the fo

articles.title: Chinese Broker’s Arrest Hits Mexico’s Fentanyl Trade Harder Than ‘Kingpin’ Captures
articles.description: A Chinese citizen accused of leading a transnational drug trafficking network with ties to Mexican criminal groups was recaptured and extradited to the United States, highlighting the key role that intermediaries play in global drug supply chains.

articles.title: Can Peru’s New President José Jerí Win His ‘War on Crime’?
articles.description: The “war on crime” proposed by Peru’s new president faces major challenges as he seeks to pull the country out of a spiraling security crisis.

articles.title: InSight Crime Co-director Weighs In on Trump’s ‘War on Drugs’
articles.description: InSight Crime Co-director Jeremy McDermott provided expert analysis to Deutsche Welle News after President Donald Trump authorized a CIA covert operation against Venezuela

articles.title: Behind the Curtain: How Trump’s Spat With Colombia Could Impact Anti-Drug Ops
articles.description: InSight Crime’s Managing Editor Deborah Bonello and Co-Director Jeremy McDermott dig into the latest war of words between President Donald Trump and his Colombian counterpart Gustavo Petro.

articles.title: On the Radar: Colombia Rejects Tren de Aragua’s Peace Proposal
articles.description: This week, we cover a mass jailbreak in Guatemala that took the authorities months to discover, and Tren de Aragua leader Larry Changa’s rejection from the government of President Gustavo Petro to talk peace.

articles.title: Tren de Aragua in Tennessee: A Ghost Train?
articles.description: In Tennessee, warnings about the advance of Tren de Aragua have been driven more by alarmism than by evidence. While the state has only seen a handful of cases, political discourse is amplifying fear and the stigmatizing of the migrant community.

articles.title: The Nashville ICE Raids: Anti-Gang Offensive or Immigration Crackdown?
articles.description: In Nashville, Tennessee, an immigration enforcement operation supposedly targeting areas with gang activity turned into a dragnet for migrants. The sweep left the migrant community gripped by fear and distrust toward authorities.

articles.title: Even as US-Mexico Border Encounters Plummet, Migrants Are Still Vulnerable to Organized Crime
articles.description: The business of smuggling migrants over the US-Mexico border is in flux, but large numbers of migrants stuck in Mexico remain at the mercy of criminal groups.

articles.title: Can Trinidad and Tobago’s New Government Tackle Gang Corruption?
articles.description: Recent measures to root out corruption in public contracts linked to gangs in Trinidad and Tobago could trigger a backlash from criminal organizations that have long profited from community development programs.

articles.title: On the Radar: Elites in the Spotlight for Criminal Ties
articles.description: Two Guyanese gold magnates indicted by a US court for fraud and money laundering, a member of Argentine President Javier Milei’s party who quit the midterm election race after admitting that he received $200,000 from an aviation tycoon wanted for drug trafficking, and the extradition from Costa Rica

articles.title: Is the Jalisco Cartel Helping the Chapitos in Mexico?
articles.description:
Reports that the Jalisco Cartel New Generation is aiding the 
Chapitos faction of Sinaloa Cartel in parts of Mexico have been growing. 
InSight Crime’s Managing Editor Deborah Bonello and Senior Investigator Parker Asmann discuss the criminal alliance, its structure, and how it contradicts some of t

articles.title: Mexico Doubles Down on Militarization With National Guard Reform
articles.description: As part of a series of security reforms pushed by Mexico’s government, the country’s main civilian public security force has been placed under military command, extending the long-standing trend of militarization in the fight against crime. InSight Crime spoke with Daira Arana, executive director of

articles.title: On the Radar: Are the Chapitos and CJNG Working Together in Mexico?
articles.description: This week, we look at a massive DEA sweep against alleged Jalisco Cartel operatives in the US, growing signs of an alliance between Mexico’s two major criminal powers, and an assassination attempt in Uruguay connected to international trafficker Sebastián Marset.

articles.title: Triple Murder Streamed Online Underscores Fears of Escalating Violence in Argentina
articles.description: The livestreamed torture and killing of three women at the hands of a drug gang has rattled Argentina and underscored the country’s vulnerability to extreme drug-fueled violence. While broadcast killings are rare in Argentina, drug trafficking groups in other Latin American countries have used

articles.description:

 The top leader of the Gaitanistas, one of Colombia’s largest criminal structures, with a presence in more than half of Colombia’s departments.

articles.description: Tren de Aragua has little chance of building criminal power in the United States with so much law enforcement and public attention focused on it.

articles.title: OAS Report Flags Short-Term Strategies to Limit LatAm Arms Trafficking

articles.description: The Border Command (Comandos de la Frontera – CDF) is a Colombian criminal group that emerged in 2017. articles.description: Alexander Díaz, alias “Calarcá Córdoba,” is the commander of the General Staff of the Blocs and Front (Estado Mayor de Bloques y Frente – EMBF), a Colombian criminal organization whose roots trace back to the country’s armed, leftist guerrilla movement.

articles.description: The General Staff of Blocks and Front (Estado Mayor de Bloques y Frente – EMBF) is a Colombian criminal organization whose roots trace back to the country’s armed, leftist guerrilla movement. It’s main criminal activities are drug trafficking, illegal mining, and extortion.

articles.description: Amid growing concerns over the spread of the screwworm parasite, Mexico’s national ranchers union is calling for tougher actions to halt the trafficking of cattle smuggled from Central America to Mexico and the United States.

articles.description: The remoteness and sparse population of Guyana’s Essequibo region makes it an attractive route for drug traffickers and a far more significant cocaine transit area than previously imagined.

articles.description: Among political threats and provocations, it is the lure of the mineral wealth of Essequibo that seems to offer the potential to ignite not only a binational security crisis between Venezuela and Guyana but a geopolitical conflict with regional implications.

articles.description: A large portion of drug seizures in Falcón, Venezuela, appear to be staged by the authorities to project an image of effectiveness in the fight against drug trafficking.

articles.description: Two historic marijuana seizures in the Dominican Republic point to an increase in the flow of the drug into the Caribbean nation. The consolidation of the local market and closer coordination among law enforcement agencies are key dynamics driving this trend.

articles.title: Global Investigative Journalism Network Notes InSight Crime’s AI Coverage
articles.description: The Global Investigative Journalism Network noted InSight Crime’s use and coverage of artificial intelligence (AI) this week in an article for World Press Freedom Day. InSight Crime increasingly employs AI […]

articles.title: As Sinaloa Cartel War Rages On, An Economy Bleeds Dry in Culiacán, Mexico
articles.description: After eight months of relentless fighting between rival Sinaloa Cartel factions, the situation has shown no signs of improving. With almost daily displays of extreme violence, Culiacán and surrounding areas are confronting historic economic losses.

articles.title: How Corruption Fuels Arms Diversion in Latin America
articles.description: Corruption is a key component of the arms trafficking networks that funnel state-owned weapons into the hands of organized crime, according to a new report.

articles.title: Transnational Organized Crime Is Feeding Growing Violence in Chile
articles.description: A first-of-its kind report has highlighted how transnational criminal groups are driving violence in Chile and eroding public security in the north of the country.

articles.title: Tren de Aragua
articles.description: The Tren de Aragua is Venezuela’s most powerful local “megabanda,” or large criminal gangs with more than 100 members. This group is largely based out of the Tocorón prison in Aragua state.

articles.title: US Asks Partners to Give More to Haiti’s Struggling Anti-Gang Mission
articles.description: The United States called on other countries to share the financial burden in fighting criminal governance and lawless chaos in Haiti, highlighting the funding struggles facing an international security support mission.

articles.description: Argentina has begun construction on its first maximum-security prison for drug traffickers and assassins near the city of Rosario, reigniting debate over the effectiveness of hardline security measures in a city plagued by drug-related violence.

articles.title: Amid Global Turmoil, Is Change Afoot in the International Drug Regime?

Or, given the US withdrawal from the world stage, it may mark the beginning of the end of the multilateral drug regime.

articles.description: Gold trafficking in Latin America and the Caribbean has never been more profitable. Prices of the precious metal surged over 40% in 2024 to reach a record high of over $3,000 per ounce in March 2025.

articles.title: Argentina Profile
articles.description: Despite Argentina’s prominent role as a money laundering center and as a drug transit and consumption point, the country does not suffer from the high levels of violence that affect other Latin American nations. articles.description: Drug trafficking accusations levied against a banana company owned by the family of Ecuador President Daniel Noboa may be politicized but raise important questions about the role of powerful business interests in the country’s wider security crisis.

articles.title: What’s Behind the Arrests of Mayors in Venezuela?
articles.description: The arrests of seven mayors in Venezuela show how President Nicolás Maduro’s government has weaponized the supposed fight against organized crime to achieve its political ends. More than a third […]

articles.title: 5 Years Later, How Has the Pandemic Changed Organized Crime in Latin America and the Caribbean?

articles.title: The US Just Unsealed Drugs and Weapons Charges Against Ecuadorian Gang Boss ‘Fito’
articles.description:
The indictment of one of Ecuador’s top criminals by a US federal court suggests that he is still directing his criminal empire while on the run, and that Ecuador is increasingly seeking US support to manage the country’s security crisis.  
 The leader of the Choneros criminal group, José Adolfo Mací

articles.title: MS13
articles.description: The Mara Salvatrucha, or MS13, is perhaps the most notorious street gang in the Western Hemisphere. articles.title: More Victims Seek Police Help With Rising Extortion in Peru
articles.description: Reports of extortion are rising again in Peru, in part because victims are more willing to seek help from the authorities.

articles.description: A series of recent arrests related to private security companies in Colombia shows how some of these businesses operate as extensions of organized crime groups and exposes yet another nexus between security forces and the criminal world.

articles.title: How to Harness Data to Fight Human Trafficking in Latin America
articles.description: Human trafficking in Latin America has evolved into a complex and diversified criminal activity, adapting to the region’s social and economic dynamics. To fight it, experts say the better use of data is key.

articles.title: Suriname Profile
articles.description: Suriname is South America’s smallest country, cocaine has moved through there since the 1980s, corruption is widespread, money laundering rampant, and its wildlife and jungle are endangered by informal mining and eco-trafficking. Read more

articles.description: Migrant smuggling dynamics in northern Colombia are shifting in response to a US immigration crackdown, potentially disrupting a lucrative revenue stream for the powerful crime group known as the Gaitanist […]

articles.title: Drones Fuel Criminal Arms Race in Latin America
articles.description: In countries like Brazil, Colombia, Mexico, and Panama, criminal groups are increasingly using drones to confront security forces, transport illicit goods, and intimidate communities.

articles.title: The Life and ‘Death’ of the Sinaloa Cartel’s Dutch Emissary: Is This Time for Real?

articles.title: Can Mexico’s President Appease Trump With Anti-Crime Crackdown?
articles.description: Authorities in Mexico have carried out a series of unprecedented security operations in a crackdown on the country’s top criminal groups as the threat of US tariffs looms. But the […]

articles.description: After four decades of successful maneuvering to avoid extradition to the United States, Mexican authorities finally put an end to the notorious criminal career of Rafael Caro Quintero.

articles.description: Ecuadorian groups are creeping into Peru, attracted by criminal economies like drug trafficking and illegal mining, and pushed by a security crackdown back home.

articles.description: The European Union has stepped up support to Latin America and the Caribbean, aware that growing alliances between European and Latin American criminal syndicates present national security threats on both sides of the Atlantic.

articles.description: For decades, Buenaventura has been the crown jewel of drug trafficking along Colombia’s Pacific corridor. articles.description: Anti-corruption advocates in Honduras are again warning of the threat of illicit campaign financing ahead of the 2025 elections, as organized crime groups continue to exploit loopholes in existing laws to influence the electoral process.

articles.description: The discovery of new evidence linked to a deceased witness in the case of Marcelo Pecci, a prominent Paraguayan anti-mafia prosecutor, has shed light on the transnational scope of those behind the assassination.

articles.description: The criminal war in northeast Colombia has escalated into a new phase of violence, as intense fighting between two Colombian guerrilla groups erupted in the Catatumbo region, a key cocaine production hub and criminal hotspot along the Venezuelan border.

articles.description: As the new Trump administration takes office, Latin America braces for shifts in US policy, with organized crime high on the agenda. articles.title: Global Pressure Mounts: Venezuela, Bounties, and Crime

articles.title: What Happened When Guatemala’s Interior Ministry Tried to Fire 102 Prison Guards It Accused of Corruption?
articles.description: In June 2024, Guatemala’s Interior Ministry filed a legal complaint with the Attorney General’s Office against 102 prison guards it accused of corruption. But prosecutors are yet to mount a robust investigation into the case, and they may have little incentive to do so.

articles.title: Paraguay’s Endless Prison Crisis
articles.description: Paraguay’s penitentiary system remains in a state of emergency for a second year, yet without measures to tackle the root causes, the relentless grip of prison gangs, both Paraguayan and Brazilian, will likely continue.

articles.title: Maduro Inauguration to Consolidate Criminal Regime in Venezuela
articles.description: After what appeared to be a stolen election, Nicolás Maduro today assumes another six-year mandate as Venezuela’s president. Heading a regime that regulates illegal economies, relying on criminal rents and partners to stay afloat, Maduro will solidify Venezuela’s position as a regional crime hub.

articles.title: Colombia’s Peace Efforts Spark Criminal Disputes and Divisions
articles.description: The prospects for peace in Colombia appear increasingly remote as illegal armed groups fragment amid a gold and cocaine bonanza. articles.title: GameChangers 2024: Narco Money Scandals Reach the Apex of Political Power in the Americas
articles.description: The year 2024 included some stark reminders that drug trafficking money can reach the highest levels of politics. From Honduras to Mexico to Colombia, allegations of narco-political contributions shook the foundations of power and disrupted international relations.

articles.title: GameChangers 2024: Networks Replace Cartels in Cocaine Trade
articles.description: As record flows of cocaine reached consumers around the globe in 2024, criminal organizations in Latin America and the Caribbean have adapted to law enforcement pressure, swapping hegemonic hierarchies for complex networks.

Extortion appears to be increasing in Honduras as smaller gangs fill the gap left by larger groups who are retreating from the criminal economy.

articles.description:

articles.title: Harrowing Massacre Underscores Depth of Gang Control in Haiti
articles.description: Reports of a chilling massacre in Haiti highlight the depth of criminal control in the country’s capital, where a largely absent state has left citizens exposed to depraved gang attacks.

articles.title: Ecuador Profile

articles.title: The Forgotten Human Smuggling Route in the Darién
articles.description: Despite its remoteness, Juradó, Colombia, is “a strategic point for everything,” and a key route for drug trafficking, arms smuggling, contraband, and migrants. InSight Crime reports from the field.

articles.title: Fito’s Fortress: How an Ecuador Prison Became a Criminal Command Center

articles.description: A recent massacre inside Ecuador’s largest and most violent prison is an ominous sign that the country’s multiple criminal organizations are atomizing at a rapid pace as the government’s hardline security crackdown stalls.

articles.title: Dominican Republic Breaks Seizure Record Amid Renewed Caribbean Trafficking

articles.description: As darkness falls over Cúcuta, Colombia’s bustling border city, shadows provide cover for criminal networks. Each night, illegal dirt tracks connecting the city to Venezuela become highways for smuggling, with large quantities of goods slipping past law enforcement.

articles.description: The massive recent seizure of cocaine precursor at Chile’s Arica port underscores its growing role as a trafficking hotspot for Bolivian drug networks.

articles.title: Turkish Broker for Sanction-Busting Venezuelan Oil Arrested
articles.description: The recent arrest of a prominent Turkish broker of Venezuelan oil in the United States may signal turbulent times ahead for Nicolás Maduro’s administration following Donald Trump’s re-election.

articles.title: Will Costa Rica Follow the Same Criminal Path as Ecuador?
articles.description: High levels of violence indicate how once-peaceful Costa Rica is feeling the tightening grip of cocaine trafficking. But is the Central American country on a similar path to Ecuador to the south?

articles.title: The Doubts Behind Bolivia’s ‘Biggest Cocaine Seizure Ever’
articles.description:

articles.title: No Limit Soldiers
articles.description: The No Limit Soldiers (NLS) is a crime group that started out as a neighborhood gang in Curaçao. The organization’s leadership set up a cocaine trafficking route to the Netherlands in the early 2000s.

articles.title: Italy’s Camorra Moves Into Colombia’s Cocaine Capital
articles.description:
Two high-ranking members of Italy’s Camorra were arrested in Medellín, Colombia, in a multinational operation against cocaine trafficking.

articles.title: Spain Reclaims Position as Cocaine Gateway to Europe
articles.description:
A record 13-ton seizure of cocaine in Spain is evidence that the country has bounced back as the main entry point into Europe for the drug.    
 The development comes after Belgium and the Netherlands significantly improved security at their ports.

articles.title: Feared Zetas Brothers Reign from Mexico Prisons: US Prosecutors
articles.description:
Authorities in the United States have accused the feared Treviño Morales brothers of continuing to control remnants of the Zetas despite a decade behind bars in Mexico.    
 The new superseding indictment raises questions as to why the infamous criminal leaders have yet to be extradited.

articles.title: People Are Overdosing on Fentanyl Pills Bought From Fake Online Pharmacies
articles.description:
US authorities recently targeted a drug trafficking ring that set up fake online pharmacies selling counterfeit prescription pills laced with fentanyl and methamphetamine.    
 The case underscores the growing sophistication of such distribution networks and the challenges of combating them.

articles.title: Cash and Violence: Organized Crime Fights to Control Brazil’s Municipal Vote
articles.description:

articles.title: Crime, Drugs, and Violence Top Concerns in Uruguay’s Election
articles.description:
As Uruguayans prepare to vote in the first round of presidential elections this weekend, security has emerged as a pivotal issue.  
 Candidates are shifting toward the center and debating whether prevention or suppression offers the best solution to escalating crime.

articles.title: Tiguerones
articles.description: Within just a few years, the Tiguerones have risen from obscurity to a position as one of Ecuador’s most dangerous and influential criminal organizations and an important link in the international cocaine trafficking supply chain.

articles.title: Arrest of Leaders of Ecuador’s Tiguerones Could Further Fracture Gang
articles.description:
The leader of the Tiguerones, one of Ecuador’s most powerful and violent criminal groups and an important link in the international cocaine supply chain, has been captured in Spain, potentially hastening the group’s fragmentation.  
 William Alcívar Bautista, alias “Willy” or “Negro Willy,” was take

articles.title: Modern Slavery in Brazil Is Targeting More Women, More Foreigners
articles.description: Brazil has seen a surge in the number of foreign nationals and women rescued from forced labor conditions, a form of human trafficking also known as modern slavery, according to a new report. articles.title: Colombia’s Coca Crops Grew, But Cocaine Production Exploded, Latest Figures Show
articles.description: Coca crops and cocaine production in Colombia increased during 2023, as the government has stopped forced eradication efforts and concentrated on seizures to fight drug trafficking.

articles.title: Mexico’s Former Security Chief Just Got Decades in Prison. But Will That Curtail Cartel Corruption?

articles.title: DEA Under Fire for Delaying Reports on Foreign Partners’ Security Abuses
articles.description:
The DEA repeatedly delayed reporting human rights violations by the Latin American security forces it was partnering with, adding to a series of scandals at the world’s most influential anti-drug agency.

articles.title: Larry Amaury Álvarez Núñez, alias ‘Larry Changa’
articles.description: Larry Amaury Álvarez Núñez, alias “Larry Changa,” is one of the co-founders and original leaders of Tren de Aragua, Venezuela’s most prominent criminal organization, which has expanded its operations across Latin America.

articles.title: Violence Against Women Is Being Used as a Weapon of War in Colombia. Again.
articles.description:
In Colombia, local criminal gangs have put women in their crosshairs because of their links to rival groups, resorting to gender-based violence as a strategy for territorial control.

articles.title: Mayor’s Beheading Is Ominous Sign of Security Crisis Facing Mexico’s New President
articles.description:
The beheading of the mayor of a major Mexican city just days after taking office has put a spotlight on the country’s dire security crisis.

articles.title: What We Know About Tren de Aragua’s US Presence
articles.description:
Tren de Aragua’s supposed expansion into the United States has received national media coverage and attention from the country’s most prominent politicians, fueling questions about the extent of the group’s presence and activities.

articles.title: El Salvador Tops Security Perceptions Survey as Chile, Ecuador Decline
articles.description: The Gallup Global Safety Report 2024 administers and analyzes a poll evaluating citizens’ experiences of violent crime (robberies, assaults, muggings), their perception of safety in general, and their trust in law enforcement over the past year. articles.title: Tren de Guayana Gang Expands Into Southern Venezuela Mining Towns
articles.description: Tren de Guayana, one of Venezuela’s oldest illegal mining groups, allegedly has used ties to President Nicolás Maduro’s administration to push out other criminal groups and solidify its power in the state of Bolívar.

articles.title: Criminals Set Their Sights on Peru’s Cultural Legacy
articles.description: The looting of archaeological heritage and land trafficking in Peru is reaching alarming levels, and authorities lack the resources and will to combat the criminal networks behind this trade. articles.description: InSight Crime continues to be a leading source of information and analysis on the presence of the Venezuelan gang Tren de Aragua in the United States. On September 20, deputy […]

articles.title: The Rise and Fall of Washo, Durán’s Criminal Puppetmaster
articles.description: In the early hours of August 14, Ecuador’s police and prosecutors deployed one of the most significant security operations of recent years in and around Durán, a coastal municipality that has become an epicenter of violence in the country.

articles.title: Tapestry of Crime and Complicity: Mapping Durán’s Power Players
articles.description: A multitude of actors play a role in security in Durán, including political, bureaucratic, and economic elites, civil society, and security forces. Corruption in the municipal government, including in the mayor’s office and various other key posts, has allowed organized crime groups to flourish. Thi

articles.title: Durán, Ecuador’s New Crime Capital
articles.description: Durán has undergone a dramatic transformation from a thriving commercial center to a violent hub of criminal activity. This shift reflects broader challenges facing Ecuador and offers critical insights into the complex interplay of social, economic, and criminal factors shaping security in the count

articles.title: Uruguay Corruption in Spotlight After Ex-Official’s Release From Prison
articles.description: Alejandro Astesiano, former head of security for ex-President of Uruguay Luis Lacalle Pou, was released from prison on September 20, two and a half years early, after serving time on organized crime charges. But as he starts a new chapter, Uruguay continues to struggle with questions about criminal

articles.title: Security Forces in Venezuela Extort Detainees Amid Political Repression
articles.description: Following the July 28 presidential elections, the Venezuelan government has carried out a massive wave of arrests. This has been accompanied by extortion, with capitalizing security officials promising not to arrest people or to release them from detention centers in exchange for money.

articles.title: Ecuador Gangs Target Prison Chiefs to Regain Control
articles.description: On the night of September 12, armed men fatally shot María Daniela Icaza, director of the Litoral Penitentiary in eastern Guayaquil, the country’s largest men’s prison and the site of some of the most harrowing massacres that have shaken Ecuador in recent years. The murder appears to be the latest a

articles.title: Narco-Subs Sail the Caribbean
articles.description: The Caribbean, historically a key region for drug trafficking, has once again become the center of attention with the seizure of a narco-submarine, signaling the use of these types of vessels is potentially becoming a more common tactic for drug shipments in this smuggling route.

articles.title: ELN Attack Destroys Chances for Peace With Strengthening Colombian Guerrilla Group
articles.description: Colombia’s government has ended peace talks with the country’s largest remaining guerrilla group after a deadly attack on a military base, effectively extinguishing the prospects for a deal with the group and leaving it stronger than when the negotiations started.

articles.title: CJNG’s Fuel Theft Empire in the Crosshairs of US Treasury Sanctions
articles.description: The U.S. Department of the Treasury has sanctioned 26 Mexican companies involved in fuel theft, signaling the growing importance of this alternative criminal economy in the finances of major organized crime groups.

articles.title: Guy Philippe
articles.description: Guy Philippe is a convicted money launderer, former police commander, and politician, who helped lead the 2004 coup against Haiti’s then-president Jean-Bertrand Aristide. After serving a six-year prison sentence in the United States, authorities repatriated Philippe to Haiti in November 2023. These

articles.title: Inside the Battle for Rio de Janeiro: Red Command Versus Militias
articles.description: In Rio, the balance of power may be shifting. Fighting intensified in 2022 when the Red Command, a notorious gang with roots in the city’s prison system, has increased its efforts to reclaim territory from the militias, a network of paramilitary-style organizations that has held sway over many

articles.title: How Ecuador’s Criminals Are Taking Advantage of Private Security Firms
articles.description: Private security companies have surged in Ecuador as crime has intensified, but mounting evidence of criminal infiltration in the industry suggests that it may be servicing criminals  as much as the private sector and the government.

articles.title: Divisions Over Peace Talks Spark Surge in Armed Activity in Huila, Colombia
articles.description: Criminal activity has escalated in a key transit corridor in southern Colombia, driven by divisions between ex-FARC factions in the area, illustrating how government peace talks appear to be roiling relations among criminal factions.

articles.title: Honduras Profile
articles.description:
Honduras, long one of the poorest countries in Latin America, is now also among the most violent and crime-ridden. The violence is carried out by local drug trafficking groups, gangs, corrupt security forces and transnational criminal organizations mainly from Mexico and Colombia. 
 In recent years,

articles.title: Ecuador’s Kidnapping Epidemic, Told by a Survivor
articles.description:
Express kidnappings are classified as “common crimes” in Ecuador, and get scant attention compared to the transnational drug trafficking that has transformed the country into one of the region’s most violent in less than a decade. 
 But crimes like kidnapping and extortion have climbed at alarming r

articles.title: Increased Foreign Presence Around Guyana Is Helping Net More Cocaine
articles.description:
Guyanese authorities recently discovered 4.4 tons of cocaine with help from the US’s DEA. 
 The enormous seizure highlights the Guyana authorities’ dependence on foreign assistance in detecting the large quantities of cocaine that pass through the country, which is a major transit hub for the drug.

articles.title: Why Venezuela’s Repression of Tren del Llano Could Feed Anti-Regime Protests
articles.description:
Gang threats against the Venezuelan regime over July’s election results handed the government an opportunity to discredit the opposition and increase military activity.    
 But the move to crack down on the Tren del Llano gang could backfire by boosting anti-government sentiment and insecurity.

articles.title: Narco Video Shows Traffickers Discussing Bribes With Honduras President’s Brother-in-Law
articles.description: InSight Crime obtained a video in which some of Honduras’ top drug traffickers met with a member of the first family and offered to give over half a million dollars to help Honduras’ ruling party and an unsuccessful run for office by now-President Xiomara Castro. The video incriminates Carlos Zelaya

articles.title: Honduras Just Killed Its Best Drug War Weapon
articles.description:
The Honduran government announced a plan to end its extradition agreement with the United States in a move that will scupper the country’s ability to tackle drug trafficking and hold crime bosses to account.  
 The move for Honduras, which is a major cocaine transit hub in the region, could be aimed

articles.title: A 3-Ton Cocaine Seizure in Venezuela Highlights Asia Trafficking Routes
articles.description:
The seizure of a three-ton cocaine shipment at Venezuela’s main airport highlights the role of travel infrastructure in drug trafficking and exposes an unusual drug trafficking route. 
 But signals that the cocaine was going to be sent to Afghanistan could be a cover for other lucrative drug t

articles.title: Zetas
articles.description: Beginning as a group of deserters from an elite unit of the armed forces at the service of the Gulf Cartel, the Zetas would go on to become one of the most powerful and feared cartels in Mexico before infighting and the loss of leaders started the organization’s decline.

articles.title: Gulf Cartel OG Who Created Mexico’s Zetas Released From US Prison
articles.description:
The United States released Osiel Cárdenas Guillén from prison after more than 20 years behind bars. 
 The former Gulf Cartel leader, who walked free today, created the Zetas and ushered in the hyper-violent organized crime wars that have marked Mexico since the turn of the century.

articles.title: Osiel Cárdenas Guillén
articles.description: Osiel Cárdenas Guillén is the former leader of the Gulf Cartel (Cartel del Golfo – CDG). At the height of his power, he was one of Mexico’s most powerful crime bosses. Cárdenas created the Zetas, which he used as an armed wing before the group outgrew its master and went its own way, ushering in an

articles.title: Inside Argentina’s Biggest Wildlife Trafficking Bust
articles.description: Caza y Safaris Argentina ran tourist packages that illegally hunted animals in Argentina. It made hundreds of thousands of dollars of illegal profits for every tour it sold, and was just shut down by the government in what could be the biggest wildlife trafficking crime ever in Argentina.

articles.title: Arrests Shed Light on Abuse of Women in Guatemala’s Prisons
articles.description:
Guatemalan authorities have made multiple busts of prison guards involved in sexual exploitation schemes that victimize women behind bars.  
 The move shows rare government action on a problem that often remains hidden, and highlights the challenge of battling corruption in the penitentiary system.

articles.title: InSight Crime’s Sinaloa Coverage: From Culiacán to the Global Press

articles.title: 4 Ways AI Is Shaping Organized Crime in Latin America
articles.description:
With access to artificial intelligence increasing rapidly, some of Latin America’s organized crime groups have begun wielding it for criminal gain, and law enforcement is struggling to keep up.

articles.title: Who Is Behind the Murder of a Powerful Colombian Emerald Dealer?
articles.description:

articles.title: After Venezuela Elections, Criminal Groups Work For or Against the Regime
articles.description:
Armed groups including colectivos, the ELN and even the criminal gang Tren del Llano have waded into post-election violence following last month’s presidential vote.

articles.description: The Gulf Cartel is among the oldest most powerful cartels in Mexico, but has lost territory in recent years.

articles.description:

articles.title: Technology Is Increasingly at the Heart of Criminal Operations in Brazil
articles.description: The recent dismantling of communication towers and illegal cellphone shops in Brazil points to the increasing use of technology by criminal groups. It also shows how victims can be targeted by aggressors both within and beyond the South American country’s borders.

articles.description:

articles.description: Record seizures of ketamine in Chile show how the drug is making its way into the country’s already extensive synthetic drug market.

articles.description: Growing rates of informal immigration in Latin America make the region increasingly vulnerable to human trafficking for organ removal.

articles.description: Costa Rican lawmakers have advanced a proposed constitutional amendment to allow the extradition of Costa Rican drug traffickers and terrorists wanted by foreign authorities, highlighting growing concern that organized crime is corrupting the judiciary’s ability to confront it.

articles.description: What is known today as the Popular Liberation Army (Ejército Popular de Liberación – EPL), or the Pelusos, is a dissident faction of the original EPL’s Libardo Mora Front that refused to demobilize with the rest of that guerrilla group in 1991. articles.title: Predictions for Venezuela’s Post-Election Criminal Landscape
articles.description: Nicolas Maduro claimed another six years in power after a disputed election, which will result in another wave of migration and Venezuela cementing its position as a regional crime hub.

articles.title: Ismael Zambada García, alias ‘El Mayo’
articles.description: Ismael Zambada Garcia, alias “El Mayo,” is battlng the Chapitos for control of the Sinaloa Cartel.

articles.description: In a stunning turn of events, two of the Sinaloa Cartel’s most-wanted drug traffickers are in custody in the United States, marking a historic moment for organized crime in Mexico but one that is unlikely to upend the flow of synthetic drugs across the border.

articles.description: This Sunday’s presidential elections will define not only the democratic future of Venezuela but its criminal evolution, and President Nicolás Maduro has deployed illegal and repressive tools in a bid to cement his power for another six years.

articles.title: Pressure Grows for Kenyan Police Fighting Haiti’s Gangs
articles.description: The second contingent of 200 Kenyan police officers arrived in Haiti on July 16, joining 200 of their compatriots who landed on June 25. As these officers work to support Haiti’s embattled police force, concerns about their ability to thwart the country’s powerful street gangs are growing.

articles.title: How Mexico’s CJNG Controls Guadalajara’s Expanding Drug Market
articles.description: One of Mexico’s most powerful crime groups, the Jalisco Cartel New Generation (Cartel Jalisco Nueva Generación – CJNG), is employing forced disappearance and corruption in its efforts to build up a burgeoning local drug market on its home turf of Guadalajara.

articles.title: Is Venezuela’s Tren de Aragua Behind Surge in Chile Kidnappings?
articles.description: A recent surge in kidnappings in Chile has coincided with Tren de Aragua, Venezuela’s most brutal prison gang, putting down roots.

articles.title: What’s Behind Falling Cocaine Seizures in the Netherlands and Belgium?
articles.description: Europe’s two main entry points for cocaine have reported a drastic drop in seizures at maritime ports during the first half of 2024, but this is likely indicative not of reduced drug flows from Latin America but rather the adaptability of transnational organized crime.

articles.title: Convictions in Ecuador Political Assassination, But No Clarity
articles.description: Last week a judge in Ecuador sentenced five people convicted in the high-profile 2023 murder of presidential candidate Fernando Villavicencio, but there is still no clarity as to who ordered the killing and why.

articles.title: Héctor Rusthenford Guerrero Flores, alias ‘Niño Guerrero’
articles.description: Hector Rusthenford Guerrero Flores, alias “Niño” Guerrero,” is the main leader of Tren de Aragua, the most important criminal group in Venezuela.

articles.title: US Labels Tren de Aragua a Transnational Criminal Threat
articles.description:
The recent US designation of the Venezuelan gang Tren de Aragua as a transnational criminal organization puts the group on par with major security threats in Latin America.  
 It also presents a new challenge to the gang’s so-far unstoppable regional expansion.

articles.title: The Mafias Behind Sand Trafficking in Latin America
articles.description:
Criminal mafias are making massive profits from the illegal extraction and sale of sand for construction purposes. 
 Illegal sand extraction causes environmental damage, and is difficult to police, since differentiating between sand extracted legally and illegally is often impossible.

articles.description: Albanian businessman Dritan Gjika allegedly built a drug trafficking empire in Ecuador that moved cocaine to Europe with the help of influential government and business elites, and protection from the chief of police.

articles.description: Brazilian cyber gangs are increasingly stealing data and holding it for ransom. Law enforcement is struggling to keep up with the explosion of cyber crimes in the country, which are hitting record highs.

articles.title: Tren de Aragua Just Sustained Its Biggest Leadership Blow
articles.description: The arrest in Colombia of one of the principal leaders of the Venezuelan criminal group Tren de Aragua represents the most significant blow to the transnational gang’s leadership to date, and opens another window for its fragmentation.

articles.title: What a Decade of Data Tells Us About Organized Crime in Brazil

articles.description: Luciano Marín Arango, alias “Iván Márquez,” is a former guerrilla commander who rejected a 2016 peace agreement and formed a dissident group known as the Second Marquetalia (Segunda Marquetalia).

articles.description:

articles.description:

In the first two months of the state of emergency, authorities arrested over 33,000 people, nearly doubling the prison population. The gangs had previously managed to exercise their power even from behind bars. But with

articles.title: ‘Too Many Soldiers’: How Bukele’s Crackdown Succeeded Where Others Failed

articles.title: The Road to El Salvador’s State of Emergency
articles.description: The road to El Salvador’s state of emergency has been long and bloody. articles.title: The Future Looks Bleak for El Salvador’s Gangs
articles.description: The gangs’ demise has radically altered the country’s criminal landscape, liberating swathes of territory and illegal markets from criminal control. articles.title: Gangs on the Run: How Bukele’s Crackdown Drove Gangs Underground
articles.description: Since the crackdown began, the gangs have shown minimal signs of armed retaliation. In a matter of months, Bukele’s crackdown had swept up tens of thousands of suspected gang members and affiliates, and the gangs’ primary reaction appears to have been to go into hiding.

articles.description: Over the last nine months, InSight Crime investigated the gangs’ response to the state of emergency and analyzed what may happen next. articles.title: Disputes Over Drug Trafficking Routes Drive Child Recruitment in Ecuador
articles.description: Many children and adolescents have been roped in by criminal gangs whose power has grown with the increase in cocaine flowing through Ecuador. articles.title: 30 Years After Escobar, How the Cocaine Trade Has Changed
articles.description: Pablo Escobar and his Medellín Cartel transformed the cocaine trade during their heyday in the 1980s. But the 30 years since the kingpin’s death have shown that the business remains one of the world’s most dynamic criminal economies.

articles.title: Johana Flores Freed After InSight Crime Investigation
articles.description: El Salvador’s armed forces arrested 24-year-old Dalila Johana Flores in January 2023. She remained imprisoned, with no way to contact either her family or her lawyer, for 309 days under […]

It was about 3:00 p.m. in the afternoon of September 16, 2021, when the Bell H1-1H, with the registration number 953, of the Honduran Air Force filled the sky over the community living on the shores of the Ébano Lagoon.  
 A few seconds later, it started to rain lead.

articles.title: El Salvador Frees ‘Terrorist’ Johana Flores
articles.description: Despite her release, Dalila Johana Flores remains a symbol of the arbitrary acts being committed under El Salvador’s state of emergency.

articles.title: 3 Takeaways From the 2023 Gallup Global Law and Order Report
articles.description: After years as the worst-ranked region, the public perception of security in Latin America and the Caribbean has improved, according to an annual global survey.  InSight Crime analyzes three cases from the 2022 report — Ecuador, El Salvador, and Venezuela — and the contexts that led to each country&

articles.title: Venezuelan Gang Leaves Trail of Dead in Roraima, Brazil

Tren de Aragua, a group known for its violent methods, advances over Brazilian territory allying itself with the First Capital Command (Primeiro Comando da Capital – PCC) and other criminal groups.

articles.description: The report, published by Human Rights Watch (HRW), describes the experiences of migrants crossing the Darién Gap, a dense and mountainous jungle region on the border between Colombia and Panama. articles.title: Brazil Profile

articles.description:

articles.description: The Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (Fuerzas Armadas Revolucionarias de Colombia — FARC) were Colombia’s largest irregular army. articles.title: Griselda Blanco, alias ‘La Madrina’
articles.description: Ana Griselda Blanco Restrepo was one of the pioneers of the cocaine trafficking pipeline from Colombia to the United States. articles.title: Fentanyl Seizures on US-Mexico Border at Record High Amid Production Ban
articles.description: Fentanyl seizures along the US-Mexico border have hit record highs, suggesting that, despite a supposed ban on production of the deadly synthetic opioid in parts of Mexico, a multitude of criminal groups are keeping production apace.

articles.title: Organized Crime Challenges Awaiting Argentina’s New President
articles.description: Javier Milei rode promises of extreme free-market reforms and hard-nosed security policies to become Argentina’s next president. But with rising insecurity, an economy in turmoil and a minority in Congress, Milei’s platform will face many barriers.
